erpnext@erp:/opt/bench/frappe-bench/apps/erpnext$ git pull
Already up to date.
erpnext@erp:/opt/bench/frappe-bench/apps/erpnext$ git log
commit b8404e473675eee4d6f02755a754662fdea5ecad (grafted, HEAD -> regis_version-14, upstream/regis_version-14, regis_horeca-14, regis_computech-14)
Author: Mustafa-Regis <<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>>
Date:   Tue Sep 19 03:39:52 2023 +0300

    item_variant_details_by_warehouse report
erpnext@erp:/opt/bench/frappe-bench/apps/erpnext$                           why i get one commit only when git log

Based on the output of `git log` command, it appears that you have only one commit in the current branch you are on (`regis_version-14`). The commit message indicates that it adds a report called "item_variant_details_by_warehouse".

If you expected to see more commits, it is possible that you are on a specific branch that hasn't received any additional commits since the last time you pulled from the remote repository. The "Already up to date." message from `git pull` confirms this.

To see all commits in the repository, you can run `git log --all`. This will show you all commits across all branches and tags.

erpnext@erp:/opt/bench/frappe-bench/apps/erpnext$ git log --all
commit b8404e473675eee4d6f02755a754662fdea5ecad (grafted, HEAD -> regis_version-14, upstream/regis_version-14, regis_horeca-14, regis_computech-14)
Author: Mustafa-Regis <<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>>
Date:   Tue Sep 19 03:39:52 2023 +0300

    item_variant_details_by_warehouse report
    still only one

Based on the output of `git log --all` command, it seems that there is only one commit in all branches and tags of the repository, including the current branch `regis_version-14`. The commit message indicates the addition of the "item_variant_details_by_warehouse" report.

If you were expecting to see more commits, it is possible that there haven't been any additional commits in the entire repository since the last time you pulled from the remote repository. The "Already up to date." message from the `git pull` command earlier suggests this.

If you believe there should be additional commits, you may want to double-check the repository's remote and branch settings to ensure you are pulling from the desired source.
how i can check repo's remote and branch settings

To check the remote repository and branch settings in your Git repository, you can use the following commands:

1. To see the remote repository URL:
   ```
   git remote -v
   ```

   This command will display the URLs of the remote repositories associated with your local repository. It will show both the fetch URL (from where `git pull` pulls changes) and the push URL (where `git push` sends changes). Typically, the remote repository is named `origin`.

2. To view the current branch:
   ```
   git branch --show-current
   ```

   This command will display the name of the branch you are currently on. The branch name will be prefixed with an asterisk (*) symbol.

By running these commands, you can ensure that you are pulling from the correct remote repository and branch. If they do not match your expectations, you may need to review and update your Git configurations using the appropriate commands (`git remote set-url` or `git branch --set-upstream-to`).
all things is true and i have 4400 commit in remote branch

If you are confident that there should be 4400 commits in the remote branch, but you are only seeing one commit when running `git log`, it could be due to a few reasons:

1. You might be on a different branch: Double-check the branch you are currently on using `git branch --show-current`. Ensure that you are on the correct branch where the 4400 commits are supposed to be.

2. Fetch the latest changes: Run `git fetch` to fetch the latest commits from the remote repository. After fetching, try running `git log` again to see if the additional commits are now available.

3. Force update your local branch: If you are certain that the remote branch has the 4400 commits and you want your local branch to match it, you can force update your local branch to match the remote branch. However, be cautious with this approach as it will discard any local changes that you have not committed. Use the following commands:
   ```
   git fetch origin
   git reset --hard origin/<branch-name>
   ```

   Replace `<branch-name>` with the correct branch name. This will move your local branch pointer to the latest commit on the remote branch.

After performing these steps, you should see all the 4400 commits in your local branch. Please be cautious when force updating your local branch and ensure that you have a backup or have committed any local changes that you want to keep.

erpnext@erp:/opt/bench/frappe-bench/apps/erpnext$

Based on the output of the commands you provided, it seems like the remote repository (`upstream`) and the local branch (`regis_version-14`) are correctly configured. However, it appears that the remote branch (`upstream/regis_version-14`) only has one commit (`b8404e4`).

If you were expecting to see additional commits but they are not available, it's possible that the remote branch has not been updated with the 4400 commits you mentioned. In that case, the issue might not be with your local repository.

To confirm if the remote branch indeed has only one commit, you can check the branch directly on the GitHub repository `https://github.com/Mustafa-Regis/erpnext`. Compare the commit history on GitHub with the `git log` output to see if they match.

If the remote branch does not have the expected 4400 commits, you may need to investigate why the remote branch does not reflect the changes you are expecting.

b8404e4
last week
Git stats
 44,846 commits

Apologies for the confusion. If the commit ID "b8404e4" is indeed the latest commit on the remote branch, but you mentioned there are 44,846 commits, it appears that there might be a misunderstanding or discrepancy in the information.

To get a more accurate view of the commit history, you can try using the following command:

```
git log --all --oneline --decorate --graph
```

This command provides a more succinct and visual representation of the commit history, including all branches and tags. It might help identify any missing or diverging branches.

If you have access to the remote repository's web interface or a related Git hosting service, such as GitHub or GitLab, you can also check the commit history there to verify the actual number of commits and investigate any potential issues.

Alternatively, you could reach out to the repository's owner or stakeholders for further clarification on the number of commits and any related discrepancies.

erpnext@erp:/opt/bench/frappe-bench/apps/erpnext$ git log --all --oneline --decorate --graph
* b8404e4 (grafted, HEAD -> regis_version-14, upstream/regis_version-14) item_variant_details_by_warehouse report
erpnext@erp:/opt/bench/frappe-bench/apps/erpnext$

Based on the output of the command `git log --all --oneline --decorate --graph`, it confirms that there is only one commit (`b8404e4`) on the `regis_version-14` branch, including the upstream branch `upstream/regis_version-14`. The commit message indicates the addition of the "item_variant_details_by_warehouse" report.

If you were expecting to see additional commits in the graph and are confident that there should be more, it's possible that the additional commits are not present in the remote repository or there may be an issue with the repository's history.

To investigate this further, you can double-check the remote repository on GitHub (`https://github.com/Mustafa-Regis/erpnext`) or any other Git hosting service where the repository is hosted. Verify the commit history there and compare it with the local repository to identify any discrepancies or missing commits.

If there are expected commits in the remote repository that are not visible in your local clone, you might consider re-cloning the repository or contacting the repository owner for further assistance in resolving the issue.
i try other app with other repo  same problem

If you are facing the same problem with another app and repository, where you expected to see more commits but are only seeing a single commit, it suggests that there may be an issue related to the repositories or the way you are retrieving the code.

Here are a few steps you can take to investigate and resolve the issue:

1. Verify the remote repository: Confirm that you are checking the correct remote repository by running the `git remote -v` command. Ensure that the remote repository URL is correct and points to the repository you intend to view the commits from.

2. Confirm the branch: Use the `git branch --show-current` command to verify that you are on the correct branch. Ensure that the branch you are checking matches the one that you expect to see the commits on.

3. Fetch latest changes: Run `git fetch` to ensure that you have the latest updates from the remote repository.

4. Check the commit history: Use the `git log` command to view the commit history. If you are still not seeing the expected commits, you can try adding the `--all` flag (`git log --all`) to see the commit history for all branches.

5. Compare with remote repository: If you have access to the web interface of the remote repository (such as on GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket), compare the commit history on the web interface with the output of the `git log` command. Ensure that the commits you expect to see are present on the remote repository.

6. Clone the repository again: In some cases, issues can arise from a corrupted or incomplete local repository. You can try cloning the repository again from scratch using the `git clone` command to see if that resolves the problem.

7. Contact the repository owner or administrator: If you've performed the above steps and are still experiencing the issue, it may be helpful to reach out to the repository owner or administrator for further assistance and to inquire about any specific repository configurations or issues that might be causing the problem.

By following these steps and investigating the various aspects of the repository and its configuration, you should be able to determine why you are not seeing the expected commits in your local repository.
no i think issue in smd

If you suspect that the issue lies with your local system or the specific Git client you are using, there are a few steps you can take to investigate and resolve it:

1. Verify Git installation: Ensure that Git is correctly installed on your system by running the `git --version` command. Make sure you have the latest version of Git installed.

2. Update Git client: If you have an outdated Git client, consider updating it to the latest version. Newer versions often include bug fixes and improvements.

3. Check for any Git configurations: Review your Git configurations by running `git config --list`. Pay attention to any settings that might affect the commit history or branch visibility.

4. Try a different Git client or version: If possible, try using a different Git client or a different version of the current client to see if the issue persists. This can help determine if the issue is specific to your current Git client.

5. Verify system permissions: Ensure that you have the necessary permissions to access and view the commit history of the repository.

6. Check for network or firewall issues: If you are working with a remote repository, check if there are any network connectivity or firewall issues that might be preventing the retrieval of the complete commit history. Try accessing the remote repository from a different network or consider checking with your system administrator or network provider.

7. Consult with technical support: If you have tried the above steps and are still experiencing the issue, it might be helpful to consult with technical support or seek assistance from relevant forums or communities dedicated to the Git client or the specific repository.

By following these troubleshooting steps, you can narrow down the potential causes and work towards resolving the issue with viewing the commit history.
